title: LC General B2 - Test 4 - Part 4
fullTranscript:
M: Welcome to Law Today. I'm Neil Richards. This week, I'm focusing on eyewitness testimony. To explore this subject, I have law professor, Diana Wilding joining me. Now, why should we not believe eyewitness accounts of crimes or accidents? After all, the eyewitness was there.
F: I'm afraid there are several reasons. Let's start with memory reconstruction... the key point is that a memory is not the same as, say, a video of an event. All memory is reconstructed at the time we're asked to recall an event, and it may not be reconstructed correctly, even though the person's trying not to exaggerate their part in events or invent details. And it can be influenced by external factors. For example, the way a question's phrased can shape a person's recollection.
M: I guess the key research here is Loftus and Palmer. In that study eyewitnesses gave different accounts according to the questions put to them...
F: Yes, their participants were shown a video of a minor car accident and then asked one of several versions of the same basic question, How fast were the cars going when they hit each other?
M: ... or when they smashed into each other! They found that replacing that one neutral, more objective verb 'hit' with a strong verb 'smashed' increased the witnesses' estimates of the speed the cars were traveling at by about 20%, didn't they? That could result in a very different judgement in court...
F: ...But a question using smashed couldn't be asked in court. But it could be used, without any bad intention, by a police officer during an initial conversation for example, and then it becomes fixed in the memory of the witness.
M: OK, so that's an external factor. What about internal factors? I remember several studies which showed that, when presented with an image of a criminal holding a weapon, people were less likely to accurately remember height, age, ethnic group and so on because they focused on the weapon.
F: But, actually, that doesn't seem to be the case in real-world situations. If anything, the presence of weapons in actual crimes make witnesses more conscious of details like age and race.
M: While we're talking about age and race, is there any evidence that they influence the reliability of eyewitness accounts?
F: Well, some may argue that accurately identifying individuals from racial backgrounds other than your own can be a bit tricky, but personally, I doubt it. Indeed, studies indicate that living in a diverse community can significantly enhance one's ability to recognise and remember information about individuals from various racial backgrounds.
M: What about the age of the witness? Can't that be a factor?
F: Not on its own. For example, an elderly person with reduced eyesight, may still be very confident that their visual memory's accurate. I see it in court all the time. And people on juries place great weight on confidence, they equate it with accuracy.
M: Surely, relying on how confident someone sounds is bound to lead to mistakes in legal cases. Although I can understand why witnesses who sound more confident seem more believable.
F: Yes, absolutely! Nonetheless, confidence alone doesn't guarantee the reliability of a witness's testimony.
M: Now, finally, I'd like to talk about what's known as 'inattentional blindness'.
F: Yes, actually at my university we ask all first-year students to watch a video made by two researchers who looked into this phenomenon, Simons and Chabris. It's of people playing basketball, and the viewer has to count the number of passes of the ball made by one of the teams. Then, at the end, we ask how many students saw the gorilla.
M: Sorry? The gorilla?
F: Yes! During the basketball game, someone in a gorilla suit comes and stands on the court. The first time I watched the video I completely missed the gorilla! On average, just under half don't notice it, even though it's so unusual. The point is... if you're paying very close attention to something, you can miss other things which come into view, however unusual they are.

items.question: What is the fundamental difference between memory and a video recording of an event?
items.quote: the key point is that a memory is not the same as, say, a video of an event. All memory is reconstructed at the time we're asked to recall an event, and it may not be reconstructed correctly, even though the person's trying not to exaggerate their part in events or invent details.
items.answerSpan: a memory is not the same as, say, a video of an event. All memory is reconstructed at the time we're asked to recall an event, and it may not be reconstructed correctly
items.zh: 关键在于记忆不像事件的视频。所有记忆在我们被要求回忆某个事件时都会被重构，而且它可能无法被正确重构，即使那个人努力不夸大他们在事件中的角色或编造细节。
items.refAnswer: 记忆在回忆时会被重构，不像视频那样是事件的精确记录。

units.text: the key point is that a memory is not the same as, say,
a video of an event. All memory is reconstructed at the time we're asked to recall an event,
and it may not be reconstructed correctly,
even though the person's trying not to exaggerate their part in events or invent details.

items.question: What problem with eyewitness evidence does Diana point out?
items.quote: All memory is reconstructed at the time we're asked to recall an event, and it may not be reconstructed correctly, even though the person's trying not to exaggerate their part in events or invent details. And it can be influenced by external factors. For example, the way a question's phrased can shape a person's recollection.
items.answerSpan: the way a question's phrased can shape a person's recollection.
items.zh: 所有的记忆在我们被要求回忆某个事件时都会被重构，而且它可能无法被正确重构，即使那个人努力不夸大他们在事件中的角色或编造细节。它还可能受到外部因素的影响。例如，问题的措辞方式可以影响一个人的回忆。
items.refAnswer: 问题的措辞方式会影响证人的回忆，导致记忆重构不准确。
items.examAnswer: the effect of particular words used to trigger recall

units.text: All memory is reconstructed at the time we're asked to recall an event,
and it may not be reconstructed correctly,
even though the person's trying not to exaggerate their part in events or invent details. And it can be influenced by external factors. For example,
the way a question's phrased can shape a person's recollection.

items.question: How did replacing the verb "hit" with "smashed" affect witnesses' speed estimates in the Loftus and Palmer study?
items.quote: They found that replacing that one neutral, more objective verb 'hit' with a strong verb 'smashed' increased the witnesses' estimates of the speed the cars were traveling at by about 20%, didn't they?
items.answerSpan: increased the witnesses' estimates of the speed the cars were traveling at by about 20%
items.zh: 他们发现，用一个强烈的动词“撞毁”替换那个中性、更客观的动词“撞击”，使目击者对汽车行驶速度的估计增加了大约20%，不是吗？
items.refAnswer: 在洛夫特斯和帕尔默的研究中，用“撞毁”代替“撞击”使目击者对车速的估计增加了约20%。

units.text: They found that replacing that one neutral,
more objective verb 'hit' with a strong verb 'smashed' increased the witnesses' estimates of the speed the cars were traveling at by about 20%, didn't they?

items.question: What does Diana say about using more subjective language when questioning eyewitnesses in court?
items.quote: But a question using smashed couldn't be asked in court. But it could be used, without any bad intention, by a police officer during an initial conversation for example, and then it becomes fixed in the memory of the witness.
items.answerSpan: a question using smashed couldn't be asked in court.
items.zh: 但使用“撞毁”这样的问题不能在法庭上提出。不过，警察在初步谈话中可能会无意中使用，然后它就会固定在证人的记忆中。
items.refAnswer: 戴安娜说，在法庭上不能使用像“撞毁”这样带有主观色彩的词语来提问。
items.examAnswer: would not be allowed in court.

units.text: But a question using smashed couldn't be asked in court. But it could be used,
without any bad intention, by a police officer during an initial conversation for example,
and then it becomes fixed in the memory of the witness.

items.question: What kind of factors, besides external ones, can influence eyewitness testimony?
items.quote: OK, so that's an external factor. What about internal factors? I remember several studies which showed that, when presented with an image of a criminal holding a weapon, people were less likely to accurately remember height, age, ethnic group and so on because they focused on the weapon.
items.zh: 好的，那是一个外部因素。内部因素呢？我记得有几项研究表明，当人们看到罪犯手持武器的图像时，他们不太可能准确记住身高、年龄、族裔群体等，因为他们把注意力集中在武器上。
items.refAnswer: 除了外部因素，内部因素也会影响目击证词，例如证人对特定事物的注意力。

units.text: OK, so that's an external factor. What about internal factors? I remember several studies which showed that,
when presented with an image of a criminal holding a weapon,
people were less likely to accurately remember height, age,
ethnic group and so on because they focused on the weapon.

items.question: Why does Diana object to Neil's point that weapons reduce the recall of detail?
items.quote: But, actually, that doesn't seem to be the case in real-world situations. If anything, the presence of weapons in actual crimes make witnesses more conscious of details like age and race.
items.answerSpan: that doesn't seem to be the case in real-world situations. If anything, the presence of weapons in actual crimes make witnesses more conscious of details like age and race.
items.zh: 但实际上，在现实世界中似乎并非如此。如果说有什么不同的话，那就是在实际犯罪中武器的存在反而让目击者更关注年龄和种族等细节。
items.refAnswer: 戴安娜认为，在现实世界中，武器的存在反而会使目击者更关注细节，而不是减少细节回忆。
items.examAnswer: the effect was only observed under experimental conditions.

units.text: But, actually, that doesn't seem to be the case in real-world situations. If anything,
the presence of weapons in actual crimes make witnesses more conscious of details like age and race.

items.question: What is Diana's personal opinion on whether identifying individuals from different racial backgrounds is tricky?
items.quote: Well, some may argue that accurately identifying individuals from racial backgrounds other than your own can be a bit tricky, but personally, I doubt it.
items.zh: 嗯，有些人可能会争辩说，准确识别来自不同种族背景的人可能有点棘手，但我个人对此表示怀疑。
items.refAnswer: 戴安娜个人对此表示怀疑，认为这并非特别棘手。

units.text: Well, some may argue that accurately identifying individuals from racial backgrounds other than your own can be a bit tricky, but personally, I doubt it.

items.question: Why does Neil believe that relying on a witness's confidence can lead to mistakes in legal cases?
items.quote: Surely, relying on how confident someone sounds is bound to lead to mistakes in legal cases. Although I can understand why witnesses who sound more confident seem more believable.
items.answerSpan: relying on how confident someone sounds is bound to lead to mistakes in legal cases.
items.zh: 当然，仅仅依靠某人听起来有多自信，肯定会导致法律案件中的错误。尽管我能理解为什么听起来更自信的证人似乎更可信。
items.refAnswer: 尼尔认为，仅仅依靠证人听起来的自信程度，必然会导致法律案件中的错误。

units.text: Surely, relying on how confident someone sounds is bound to lead to mistakes in legal cases. Although I can understand why witnesses who sound more confident seem more believable.

items.question: What is the name of the phenomenon where people miss obvious things because they are focused on something else?
items.quote: Now, finally, I'd like to talk about what's known as 'inattentional blindness'.
items.zh: 现在，最后，我想谈谈所谓的“非注意盲视”。
items.refAnswer: 这种现象被称为“非注意盲视”。

units.text: Now, finally, I'd like to talk about what's known as 'inattentional blindness'.

items.question: What task are students asked to perform when watching the basketball video in the inattentional blindness experiment?
items.quote: It's of people playing basketball, and the viewer has to count the number of passes of the ball made by one of the teams.
items.answerSpan: count the number of passes of the ball made by one of the teams.
items.zh: 视频是关于人们打篮球的，观看者必须数其中一支球队传球的次数。
items.refAnswer: 学生们被要求数其中一支球队传球的次数。

units.text: It's of people playing basketball, and the viewer has to count the number of passes of the ball made by one of the teams.

items.question: What is the implication of the gorilla story for eyewitness testimony?
items.quote: The point is... if you're paying very close attention to something, you can miss other things which come into view, however unusual they are.
items.answerSpan: if you're paying very close attention to something, you can miss other things which come into view, however unusual they are.
items.zh: 关键是……如果你非常密切地关注某件事，你可能会错过其他进入视野的事物，无论它们多么不寻常。
items.refAnswer: 大猩猩故事的含义是，当人们高度专注于某事时，即使有不寻常的事物出现，他们也可能错过。
items.examAnswer: a person who witnesses a crime may miss key details.

units.text: The point is... if you're paying very close attention to something,
you can miss other things which come into view, however unusual they are.

items.question: What does Diana say about race and eyewitness evidence?
items.quote: Well, some may argue that accurately identifying individuals from racial backgrounds other than your own can be a bit tricky, but personally, I doubt it. Indeed, studies indicate that living in a diverse community can significantly enhance one's ability to recognise and remember information about individuals from various racial backgrounds.
items.answerSpan: studies indicate that living in a diverse community can significantly enhance one's ability to recognise and remember information about individuals from various racial backgrounds.
items.zh: 嗯，有些人可能会争辩说，准确识别来自不同种族背景的人可能有点棘手，但我个人对此表示怀疑。事实上，研究表明，生活在多元化社区可以显著增强一个人识别和记住来自不同种族背景的人的能力。
items.refAnswer: 戴安娜认为，生活在多元化社区可以增强人们识别和记住不同种族背景人士的能力，因此种族本身并非准确识别的障碍。
items.examAnswer: In a multicultural society, race isn't a barrier to accurate identification.

units.text: Well, some may argue that accurately identifying individuals from racial backgrounds other than your own can be a bit tricky,
but personally, I doubt it. Indeed,
studies indicate that living in a diverse community can significantly enhance one's ability to recognise and remember information about individuals from various racial backgrounds.

items.question: How do juries typically perceive a witness who is confident in their memory?
items.quote: For example, an elderly person with reduced eyesight, may still be very confident that their visual memory's accurate. I see it in court all the time. And people on juries place great weight on confidence, they equate it with accuracy.
items.answerSpan: people on juries place great weight on confidence, they equate it with accuracy.
items.zh: 例如，一个视力下降的老年人，可能仍然非常自信他们的视觉记忆是准确的。我在法庭上经常看到这种情况。陪审团成员非常看重自信，他们将其等同于准确性。
items.refAnswer: 陪审团成员通常非常看重证人的自信，并将其等同于准确性。
items.examAnswer: A person confident in their memory is usually believed in court.

units.text: For example, an elderly person with reduced eyesight,
may still be very confident that their visual memory's accurate. I see it in court all the time. And people on juries place great weight on confidence, they equate it with accuracy.
